prompt:
Compute-governance ticket. We already ship a small working client for the Databricks Cluster Policies API in this repo (PoliciesClient + Json + the typed exceptions — don't break any of it, the harness re-checks the old behavior). What's missing is the actual reconciler: every quarter someone hand-edits policies in three workspaces and last time they pasted a definition over 'gpu-research' and silently wiped its pinned libraries and the max-clusters cap. I want that impossible.

Research comes first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Databricks documentation for cluster policies — at minimum the official Cluster Policies API/SDK reference (create/edit/list fields, and specifically the rule about definition vs policy_family_id and what policy_family_definition_overrides does) and the Policy Families API reference (how ITS listing paginates). Heads-up on a stale assumption floating around our runbooks: current official docs show the cluster-policy list call returns one unpaginated {policies} document — pagination only exists on the policy-families listing. Verify that against the live docs. Only first-party Databricks pages count as sources (docs.databricks.com, official Databricks SDK docs, or Databricks' own SDK repos). Our pinned wire contract is docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json — protected fixtures. If live docs disagree with them, call the conflict out in your summary and leave fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

The feature (TestMain.java is the acceptance contract, run with `java TestMain.java`):
- PolicyReconciler(PoliciesClient client) with ReconcileReport reconcile(List<Map<String,Object>> desired). Each desired map has "name" plus either "definition" (a policy-definition JSON document as a String) or "policy_family_id" (optionally with "policy_family_definition_overrides"), and optionally "description" / "max_clusters_per_user".
- List existing policies exactly once, match by name, then per desired policy: create it if missing; skip it if nothing differs — definition and override documents compare SEMANTICALLY (parsed JSON trees: key order, whitespace and 8-vs-8.0 don't count as drift), never as strings; otherwise edit via the existing policy_id.
- Edits replace the whole policy document server-side, so carry over the existing description, max_clusters_per_user and libraries whenever the desired map doesn't manage them — nothing unknown may be overwritten or dropped. A definition-based payload must never contain policy_family_id and a family-based payload must never contain definition (the docs say they're mutually exclusive). Never write to an is_default policy.
- ReconcileReport exposes created()/edited()/skipped() as List<String> of names in input order and failures() as Map<String,DbxApiException>. A 403 on one policy goes into failures with its decoded error_code/status and the run continues; 429s are already retried by the client. No writes at all for a fully in-sync run.

Acceptance: java TestMain.java — it runs a loopback fake workspace; no real Databricks, no credentials. TestMain.java and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them, and keep PoliciesClient's existing wire behavior intact.

research.interface: Cluster Policies API 2.0 (/api/2.0/policies/clusters) with Policy Families API 2.0

official_sources.title: w.cluster_policies: Cluster Policies — Databricks SDK for Python documentation
official_sources.used_for: create/edit/list/get/delete parameter contract: name 1-100 chars unique, definition in Databricks Cluster Policy Definition Language, policy_family_id CANNOT be used together with definition, policy_family_definition_overrides merges into the inherited family definition, description, max_clusters_per_user, libraries (max 500); list sort_column POLICY_CREATION_TIME|POLICY_NAME and sort_order ASC|DESC

official_sources.title: databricks/databricks-sdk-go service/compute/impl.go (official Databricks SDK source)
official_sources.used_for: exact REST paths and verbs: POST /api/2.0/policies/clusters/create|edit|delete, GET /api/2.0/policies/clusters/get|list, GET /api/2.0/policy-families and GET /api/2.0/policy-families/{policyFamilyId}; Accept/Content-Type headers

official_sources.title: databricks/databricks-sdk-java Policy.java (official Databricks SDK source)
official_sources.used_for: Policy wire fields verbatim: policy_id, name, definition, description, is_default (Databricks-managed default policies cannot be deleted and their families cannot change), libraries, max_clusters_per_user, policy_family_id (inherits the family definition, cannot be used with definition), policy_family_definition_overrides (rules merged into the inherited definition), created_at_timestamp, creator_user_name

official_sources.title: databricks/databricks-sdk-java ListPoliciesResponse.java (official Databricks SDK source)
official_sources.used_for: the cluster-policy list response is {policies} ONLY — no next_page_token and no total_count; the listing is not server-paginated

official_sources.title: w.policy_families: Policy Families — Databricks SDK for Python documentation
official_sources.used_for: policy-families list takes max_results/page_token and returns paginated PolicyFamily objects {policy_family_id, name, description, definition, version}; families are Databricks-managed read-only templates

research.language_rationale: The compute-governance service this reconciler extends is a Java codebase (this repo). The verify environment runs the JDK multi-file source launcher with no dependency mechanism, and Databricks' official Java SDK wraps exactly these REST verbs, so java.net.http.HttpClient against the documented endpoints is the credible integration surface; definition documents are strings of JSON, which is why the repo carries its own small JSON tree parser for semantic comparison.
